lesson.title: GroupBy & Merge
lesson.body: GroupBy applies the split-apply-combine paradigm: split the DataFrame into groups, apply a function, combine results. df.groupby('col').agg({'val': 'mean'}) is the most common pattern. pd.merge() joins DataFrames like SQL: inner (only matching rows), left (all left rows), right, or outer (all rows). pd.concat() stacks DataFrames vertically (axis=0) or horizontally (axis=1).

rooms.question: Complete the code to compute the mean salary by department:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'dept':['A','B','A','B'],'salary':[50,60,55,65]})
result = df.___(  'dept')['salary'].mean()
rooms.explanation: df.groupby('dept')['salary'].mean() — groupBy creates groups by department, then computes mean salary within each group.

rooms.question: Complete the code to merge two DataFrames on 'id':
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df1 = pd.DataFrame({'id':[1,2,3],'name':['A','B','C']})
df2 = pd.DataFrame({'id':[2,3,4],'score':[90,85,88]})
result = pd.___(df1, df2, on='id', how='inner')
rooms.explanation: pd.merge(left, right, on='key', how='type') performs SQL-style joins. 'inner' keeps only rows where 'id' appears in both DataFrames.

rooms.question: A left join (how='left') in pd.merge() keeps:
answers: All rows from the left DataFrame, NaN for non-matching right rows
All rows from the right DataFrame
rooms.explanation: Left join: all left rows are preserved. Where there's no match in the right DataFrame, NaN values fill the right columns.

rooms.question: Order the GroupBy aggregation steps (split-apply-combine):
items: Combine: results are assembled into a new DataFrame
Optional: .reset_index() if you want flat column index
rooms.explanation: Split → Apply → Combine is the Pandas groupby paradigm. reset_index() is often needed when the group-by keys become the index.

rooms.question: Complete the code to stack two DataFrames vertically:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df1 = pd.DataFrame({'a':[1,2],'b':[3,4]})
df2 = pd.DataFrame({'a':[5,6],'b':[7,8]})
result = pd.___(  [df1, df2], ignore_index=True)
rooms.explanation: pd.concat([df1, df2]) stacks DataFrames vertically (default axis=0). ignore_index=True resets the index to 0,1,2,... in the result.

boss.name: The Merge Conflict
boss.taunt: Your keys don't match. Your NaNs are everywhere. Welcome to real data.

questions.question: Complete the code to apply multiple aggregations at once:
questions.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'dept':['A','B','A'],'score':[80,90,70]})
result = df.groupby('dept')['score'].___({'score_mean': 'mean', 'score_max': 'max'})
questions.explanation: groupby().agg(dict) computes multiple aggregations in one pass. Result columns are named by the dictionary keys.

questions.question: What is the difference between pd.merge() and pd.concat()?
answers: merge() aligns on key columns (join); concat() stacks DataFrames by axis
merge() only works for inner joins
questions.explanation: merge() = SQL join (align on key column values). concat() = stack (bind rows or columns). They serve different purposes.

questions.question: Complete to get the size of each group:
questions.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'team':['A','B','A','A','B'],'val':[1,2,3,4,5]})
sizes = df.groupby('team').___()
questions.explanation: groupby().size() returns the number of rows per group. .count() is similar but excludes NaN values per column.
